## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/duhl/git/anedot-frontend

<INSTRUCTIONS>
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</INSTRUCTIONS>

Use `git diff HEAD~1` to see my latest commit then perform a focused code review
of the following changes.

Review goals:

- Identify the most important bugs, edge cases, or logical pitfalls
- Highlight small, high-leverage improvements that make the code easier to read, reason about, or modify
- Evaluate whether the changes correctly meet the intended business requirements and user impact

Constraints:

- Do NOT suggest large refactors or architectural changes
- Do NOT comment on linting, formatting, naming style, or test coverage
- Do NOT propose changes unrelated to the intent of the diff
- Assume existing patterns and structure are intentional unless clearly harmful

Review style:

- Prioritize severity and impact over exhaustiveness
- Explain why an issue matters (risk, correctness, maintainability)
- Be concise, direct, and professional (principal-level engineer tone)
- Prefer actionable suggestions over abstract critique

Output format:

- Use bullet points
- Group feedback by severity: Critical, Important, Nice to have
- If no issues exist in a category, omit it

payload.text:
**Reviewing date handling and potential bugs**

I'm checking how dates are passed, especially undefined or null values, and whether formatDateInTimeZone handles optional or nullable inputs safely. I want to understand if removing toISOString affects timezone shifts and if the current date parsing could cause wrong dates for users in different time zones. There's also a need to verify how futurePledgeDate flows through components and if any type mismatches might cause visual issues like showing 'N/A'. I'll look deeper into formatDate.ts to clarify these behaviors.

summary.text:
**Reviewing date handling and potential bugs**

I'm checking how dates are passed, especially undefined or null values, and whether formatDateInTimeZone handles optional or nullable inputs safely. I want to understand if removing toISOString affects timezone shifts and if the current date parsing could cause wrong dates for users in different time zones. There's also a need to verify how futurePledgeDate flows through components and if any type mismatches might cause visual issues like showing 'N/A'. I'll look deeper into formatDate.ts to clarify these behaviors.

payload.text:
**Analyzing time zone and date handling risks**

I’m carefully examining how date formatting and comparisons interact with account time zones—especially around future pledge dates. The key concern is that while display uses account time zones, the visibility logic compares absolute times, which usually aligns but can cause shifts for date-only strings parsed as UTC midnights. This leads to preview regressions where dates may appear a day earlier or later depending on the viewer's timezone. I found this mismatch significant and will highlight it along with minor edge cases like fallback formats ignoring time zones and prop type looseness. Overall, this feels like a nuanced but important review focusing on timezone-induced shifts affecting UI consistency.

**Reporting preview date shift issue**

I’m pinpointing an important issue where builder preview components now pass Date objects derived from date-only strings into formatDateInTimeZone, causing date shifts for time zones west of UTC. This happens because formatInTimeZone interprets those Dates as UTC midnights, so the displayed date can be one day earlier than expected. It’s triggered by removing toISOString conversions in FinanceBlockPreview and LiveFinancePreview. I’ll highlight this with file paths and suggest passing full ISO strings or formatting without time zone for date-only values. No critical issues found.

summary.text:
**Analyzing time zone and date handling risks**

I’m carefully examining how date formatting and comparisons interact with account time zones—especially around future pledge dates. The key concern is that while display uses account time zones, the visibility logic compares absolute times, which usually aligns but can cause shifts for date-only strings parsed as UTC midnights. This leads to preview regressions where dates may appear a day earlier or later depending on the viewer's timezone. I found this mismatch significant and will highlight it along with minor edge cases like fallback formats ignoring time zones and prop type looseness. Overall, this feels like a nuanced but important review focusing on timezone-induced shifts affecting UI consistency.

**Reporting preview date shift issue**

I’m pinpointing an important issue where builder preview components now pass Date objects derived from date-only strings into formatDateInTimeZone, causing date shifts for time zones west of UTC. This happens because formatInTimeZone interprets those Dates as UTC midnights, so the displayed date can be one day earlier than expected. It’s triggered by removing toISOString conversions in FinanceBlockPreview and LiveFinancePreview. I’ll highlight this with file paths and suggest passing full ISO strings or formatting without time zone for date-only values. No critical issues found.

payload.message: - Important: `formatDateInTimeZone` now formats `futurePledgeDate` using account TZ while previews pass a Date object derived from `parseISO(configuration.futurePledgeDate.split('T')[0])`; for time zones west of UTC this Date represents the prior day at local time, so `formatDateInTimeZone` can show the wrong date (e.g., 03/09 instead of 03/10) in previews compared to the saved value and public page. This regression comes from dropping the `toISOString()` conversion in `src/pages/ActionPages/pages/ActionPagesBuilder/components/Preview/FinanceBlockPreview/FinanceBlockPreview.tsx` and `src/pages/ActionPages/pages/ActionPagesBuilder/components/Preview/LivePreview/LiveFinancePreview/LiveFinancePreview.tsx` combined with the new time-zone formatting in `src/ui/utils/formatDate/formatDate.ts`. Consider passing the original ISO string with time zone (or normalizing the Date to account TZ before formatting) so previews don’t shift by a day.

content.text: - Important: `formatDateInTimeZone` now formats `futurePledgeDate` using account TZ while previews pass a Date object derived from `parseISO(configuration.futurePledgeDate.split('T')[0])`; for time zones west of UTC this Date represents the prior day at local time, so `formatDateInTimeZone` can show the wrong date (e.g., 03/09 instead of 03/10) in previews compared to the saved value and public page. This regression comes from dropping the `toISOString()` conversion in `src/pages/ActionPages/pages/ActionPagesBuilder/components/Preview/FinanceBlockPreview/FinanceBlockPreview.tsx` and `src/pages/ActionPages/pages/ActionPagesBuilder/components/Preview/LivePreview/LiveFinancePreview/LiveFinancePreview.tsx` combined with the new time-zone formatting in `src/ui/utils/formatDate/formatDate.ts`. Consider passing the original ISO string with time zone (or normalizing the Date to account TZ before formatting) so previews don’t shift by a day.
